pathways.label: Adult conviction expungement under Wis. Stat. § 973.015
pathways.summary: For most adult criminal convictions, Wilma should check all of these: Age Person was under 25 at the time of the offense Offense severity Maximum imprisonment for the offense was 6 years or less Timing of order Court ordered expungement at sentencing Court finding Court found the person would benefit and society would not be harmed

ruleClauses: For most adult criminal convictions, Wilma should check all of these:
Age Person was under 25 at the time of the offense
Offense severity Maximum imprisonment for the offense was 6 years or less
Timing of order Court ordered expungement at sentencing
Court finding Court found the person would benefit and society would not be harmed
Successful completion No subsequent conviction before completion; probation not revoked; probation conditions satisfied
Sentence completion Sentence must be fully completed before expungement takes effect

Wisconsin 973.015 allows the sentencing court to order expungement at the time of sentencing for a person under 25 whose offense carries a maximum imprisonment of 6 years or less, if the court finds the person will benefit and society will not be harmed. (
"https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/")
Mandatory youthful invasion-of-privacy route

If the offense was certain invasion-of-privacy conduct under Wis. Stat. 942.08(2)(b), (c), (d), or (3) and the person was under 18 when it was committed, the court shall order expungement at sentencing upon successful completion. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/")

For Class H felonies, the court may not order expungement if the person has a prior felony, if the offense is a violent offense, or if the offense is stalking, certain child-abuse offenses, or sexual assault by school staff. For Class I felonies, the court may not order expungement if the person has a prior felony, if the offense is violent, or if the offense is concealing the death of a child. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/")
The at sentencing trap This is the biggest Wisconsin issue.
At your sentencing hearing, did the judge specifically order that the record would be expunged after successful completion of the sentence?
If the answer is yes, Wilma checks sentence completion and the correct execution path.
If the answer is no, Wilma should say:

Wisconsin usually requires adult expungement to be ordered at sentencing. If it was not ordered then, there may not be a later petition route for that conviction, unless a special trafficking-victim route applies.
Wisconsin case law cited under 973.015 states that
means when sentence is imposed, and that nothing in the statute lets the circuit court revisit the expungement decision later. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/")
Wisconsin non-convictions are not handled through 973.015 adult conviction expungement. They route through DOJ-CIB fingerprint/arrest removal.
A person may qualify for arrest fingerprint record removal if they were:
arrested and released without charge;
had the charges dismissed by the court or prosecutor;
or no charges were filed by the prosecutor.

Wisconsin DOJ says the request uses DJ-LE-250B, requires fingerprints, has no fee, and if the removed Wisconsin arrest information had been forwarded to the FBI, the FBI will be contacted to remove it from the FBI record.
Wilma caution: All charges from that arrest event must be cleared. If one charge from the same arrest resulted in conviction, DOJ-CIB removal may be blocked.
For Wisconsin, train Wilma with this core logic:

Wisconsin expungement is narrow.Adult conviction expungement must usually be ordered at sentencing.Under 25 + max imprisonment 6 years or less is only the starting point; the sentencing order is the make-or-break fact.Successful completion means no subsequent conviction, no probation revocation, and all probation conditions satisfied.CR-266 is only for adult conviction cases where expungement was already ordered and there was no probation/incarceration.Dismissed/no-file/not-guilty arrests route to DOJ-CIB fingerprint removal, not court expungement.Juvenile adjudication expungement uses JD-1780 after age 17 and completed disposition.Trafficking-victim prostitution relief is a special manual-review route.Never promise that Wisconsin expungement removes DOJ-CIB, police, DA, DOT, FBI, or private-background records.

Stat. 938.355(4m) for juvenile adjudication expungement; Wis. Stat. 165.84 / DOJ-CIB process for removal of arrest fingerprint records.Core warning: Wisconsin is one of the narrowest expungement states. Adult expungement usually must be ordered at sentencing. If the judge did not order expungement at sentencing, Wilma should treat the adult conviction as likely not expungeable later, unless the case fits the human-trafficking/prostitution route. Wisconsin case law says the expungement decision under 973.015 must be made at sentencing, and courts generally cannot revisit it later. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/")
Adult conviction, user was under 25, but judge did not order expungement at sentencing Usually no adult expungement route Manual review only; court generally cannot add it later

This is separate from court expungement. Wisconsin DOJ says arrest fingerprint information may be removed when the person was released without charge or cleared through court proceedings, but a conviction ordered expunged by a court cannot be removed from the Wisconsin criminal history repository because the conviction disqualifies that arrest for removal.

Wisconsin case law says the expungement decision under 973.015 must be made at sentencing, and courts generally cannot revisit it later. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/") 1.

pathways.label: Human-trafficking / prostitution relief under § 973.015(2m)
pathways.summary: Wisconsin has a special route for people convicted, adjudicated delinquent, or found not guilty by reason of mental disease or defect for prostitution under 944.30, if the offense was committed as a result of being a victim of trafficking for purposes of a commercial sex act. The person may move the court to vacate the conviction/adjudication/finding or expunge the record if: the person was a trafficking victim for commercial sex; the 944.30 violation resulted from that trafficking; the motion includes facts and may include court records, immigration-related certifications, official government documentation, or other credible evidence;
ruleClauses: Wisconsin has a special route for people convicted, adjudicated delinquent, or found not guilty by reason of mental disease or defect for prostitution under 944.30, if the offense was committed as a result of being a victim of trafficking for purposes of a commercial sex act.
The person may move the court to vacate the conviction/adjudication/finding or expunge the record if:
the person was a trafficking victim for commercial sex;
the 944.30 violation resulted from that trafficking;
the motion includes facts and may include court records, immigration-related certifications, official government documentation, or other credible evidence;
the motion was made with due diligence, considering safety concerns;
the district attorney is served or notified and has an opportunity to respond;
the court finds the person will benefit and society will not be harmed. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/")
Wilma trauma rule: Do not force details. Ask only:
Was this prostitution-related case connected to trafficking, coercion, exploitation, survival circumstances, or someone forcing or directing you?
Then route to manual/legal review.

pathways.label: Juvenile-adjudication expungement under Wis. Stat. § 938.355(4m)
pathways.summary: A juvenile adjudicated delinquent may petition after reaching age 17 to expunge the court record of the adjudication. The court may expunge if the juvenile satisfactorily completed the dispositional order, the juvenile will benefit, and society will not be harmed. ("https://docs.legis.wisconsin.gov/statutes/statutes/938/VI/355/4m?view=section&utm_source=chatgpt.com") The official juvenile form is JD-1780 - Petition to Expunge Court Record of Adjudication / Recommendation of District Attorney. The form summary explains that a juvenile adjudicated delinquent after reaching age 17 may petition, and that the court looks at satisfactory…
pathways.sourceRef: section:109-112; section:237-239; corpus-unit:5; corpus-unit:45; summary:237-239; corpus-unit:93
ruleClauses: A juvenile adjudicated delinquent may petition after reaching age 17 to expunge the court record of the adjudication. The court may expunge if the juvenile satisfactorily completed the dispositional order, the juvenile will benefit, and society will not be harmed. ("https://docs.legis.wisconsin.gov/statutes/statutes/938/VI/355/4m?view=section&utm_source=chatgpt.com")

The official juvenile form is JD-1780 - Petition to Expunge Court Record of Adjudication / Recommendation of District Attorney. The form summary explains that a juvenile adjudicated delinquent after reaching age 17 may petition, and that the court looks at satisfactory completion, benefit to the juvenile, and whether society would be harmed. ("https://www.wicourts.gov/forms1/circuit/ccform.jsp?FormNumber=JD-1780") ("https://www.wicourts.gov/formdisplay/JD-1780_summary.pdf?formNumber=JD-1780&formType=Summary&formatId=2&language=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com")

Wilma caution: Juvenile expungement removes the courts record of adjudication, but juvenile records are already treated differently from adult public CCAP/WCCA records. Do not oversell it as wiping every agency record.

sourceSections.title: Remedy definitions Wilma must understand
sourceSections.text:
Wisconsin expungement
Wisconsin expungement usually removes/seals the court record of the conviction from public court access. It does notvacate the conviction, set it aside, or erase every government record. The official CR-266 form warns that only the court record of conviction is expunged; police, Crime Information Bureau, law enforcement, and district attorney records are not affected, and the conviction is not vacated or set aside. (

"https://www.wicourts.gov/formdisplay/CR-266.pdf?formNumber=CR-266&formType=Form&formatId=2&language=en")
DOJ-CIB arrest fingerprint removal
This is separate from court expungement. Wisconsin DOJ says arrest fingerprint information may be removed when the person was released without charge or cleared through court proceedings, but a conviction ordered expunged by a court cannot be removed from the Wisconsin criminal history repository because the conviction disqualifies that arrest for removal.
Wilma rule: In Wisconsin, never say
your record will be wiped from everywhere.
Say:
The court record may be expunged from public court access, but DOJ-CIB, police, prosecutor, DOT, FBI, and private-background records may require separate review or may remain.

sourceSections.title: Adult conviction expungement - Wis. Stat. 973.015
sourceSections.text:
Basic eligibility
For most adult criminal convictions, Wilma should check all of these:

Requirement Wisconsin rule
Age Person was under 25 at the time of the offense
Offense severity Maximum imprisonment for the offense was 6 years or less
Timing of order Court ordered expungement at sentencing
Court finding Court found the person would benefit and society would not be harmed
Successful completion No subsequent conviction before completion; probation not revoked; probation conditions satisfied
Sentence completion Sentence must be fully completed before expungement takes effect
Wisconsin 973.015 allows the sentencing court to order expungement at the time of sentencing for a person under 25 whose offense carries a maximum imprisonment of 6 years or less, if the court finds the person will benefit and society will not be harmed. (

"https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/")
Mandatory youthful invasion-of-privacy route
If the offense was certain invasion-of-privacy conduct under Wis. Stat. 942.08(2)(b), (c), (d), or (3) and the person was under 18 when it was committed, the court shall order expungement at sentencing upon successful completion. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/")
Felony exclusions
For Class H felonies, the court may not order expungement if the person has a prior felony, if the offense is a violent offense, or if the offense is stalking, certain child-abuse offenses, or sexual assault by school staff. For Class I felonies, the court may not order expungement if the person has a prior felony, if the offense is violent, or if the offense is concealing the death of a child. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/")
5. The
at sentencing
trap
This is the biggest Wisconsin issue.
Wilma should ask:

At your sentencing hearing, did the judge specifically order that the record would be expunged after successful completion of the sentence?

If the answer is yes, Wilma checks sentence completion and the correct execution path.
If the answer is no, Wilma should say:

Wisconsin usually requires adult expungement to be ordered at sentencing. If it was not ordered then, there may not be a later petition route for that conviction, unless a special trafficking-victim route applies.

Wisconsin case law cited under 973.015 states that
at the time of sentencing
means when sentence is imposed, and that nothing in the statute lets the circuit court revisit the expungement decision later. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/wisconsin/chapter-973/section-973-015/")

sourceSections.title: Adult non-conviction / arrest-only records
Human-trafficking / prostitution relief - 973.015(2m)
Juvenile adjudication expungement - Wis. Stat. 938.355(4m)
sourceSections.text:
A juvenile adjudicated delinquent may petition after reaching age 17 to expunge the court record of the adjudication. The court may expunge if the juvenile satisfactorily completed the dispositional order, the juvenile will benefit, and society will not be harmed. ("https://docs.legis.wisconsin.gov/statutes/statutes/938/VI/355/4m?view=section&utm_source=chatgpt.com")
The official juvenile form is JD-1780 - Petition to Expunge Court Record of Adjudication / Recommendation of District Attorney. The form summary explains that a juvenile adjudicated delinquent after reaching age 17 may petition, and that the court looks at satisfactory completion, benefit to the juvenile, and whether society would be harmed. ("https://www.wicourts.gov/forms1/circuit/ccform.jsp?FormNumber=JD-1780") ("https://www.wicourts.gov/formdisplay/JD-1780_summary.pdf?formNumber=JD-1780&formType=Summary&formatId=2&language=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma caution: Juvenile expungement removes the courts record of adjudication, but juvenile records are already treated differently from adult public CCAP/WCCA records. Do not oversell it as wiping every agency record.

sourceSections.title: Pardon route
sourceSections.text:
A Wisconsin pardon is separate from expungement. Wisconsin DOJ says a pardon will not remove the arrest from the criminal history record; the disposition will show that a Governors pardon was issued and may restore certain rights.
Wilma rule: If the user says
I was pardoned,
do not say the record is expunged. Route to pardon notation, rights restoration, and criminal-history update review.

sourceSections.title: Waiting-period table
sourceSections.text:
Route Timing
Adult 973.015 expungement Must be ordered at sentencing; takes effect after successful sentence completion
Adult conviction with probation/incarceration Certificate of discharge triggers court expungement after successful completion
Adult conviction with no probation/incarceration Use CR-266 after successful completion, if expungement was ordered at sentencing
Youthful invasion-of-privacy mandatory route Ordered at sentencing if statutory criteria met; effective after successful completion
Trafficking-victim prostitution route Motion may be filed after conviction/adjudication/NGI finding, subject to due-diligence and safety considerations
DOJ-CIB arrest fingerprint removal After release without charge, no-file, dismissal, acquittal, or other clearing of all charges from arrest event
Juvenile adjudication Petition after reaching age 17 and satisfactorily completing dispositional order

sourceSections.title: Filing workflow
sourceSections.text:
Adult conviction where expungement was ordered at sentencing

1 Confirm the sentencing court ordered expungement.
2 Confirm sentence was successfully completed.
3 Confirm no subsequent conviction occurred before completion.
4 If probation/incarceration existed, verify the certificate of discharge was sent to the clerk.
5 If no probation/incarceration existed, prepare CR-266 and proposed CR-267.
6 File in the same circuit court case.
7 Confirm the court record is sealed/removed from WCCA/CCAP public access.
Arrest-only / dismissed / not guilty / no-file
1 Get the DOJ criminal-history record.
2 Confirm the arrest event and all charges tied to that fingerprint card.
3 Collect disposition/no-prosecution documents.
4 Complete DJ-LE-250B with required fingerprint.
5 Submit to Wisconsin DOJ-CIB.
6 If FBI record was involved, confirm DOJ transmitted the removal.
Juvenile adjudication
1 Confirm age 17 or older.
2 Confirm dispositional order completed.
3 Prepare JD-1780.
4 Seek district attorney recommendation if required by local practice/form.
5 File in the juvenile court case.
6 Attend hearing if required.

sourceSections.title: Wilma intake decision tree
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Adult conviction, juvenile adjudication, or non-conviction?
Ask:

Was this an adult conviction, juvenile adjudication, dismissed/no-file/not-guilty arrest, or are you not sure?

Step 2 - If adult conviction
Ask:

Were you under 25 when the offense happened, and did the judge order expungement at sentencing?

If no order at sentencing, likely no standard adult route.If yes, check successful completion.
Step 3 - If non-conviction
Ask:

Were all charges from that arrest dismissed, not filed, or resolved not guilty?

If yes, route to DOJ-CIB DJ-LE-250B.If one charge resulted in conviction, fingerprint removal may be blocked.
Step 4 - If juvenile
Ask:

Are you at least 17, and did you complete the dispositional order?

If yes, route to JD-1780.
Step 5 - If prostitution / trafficking
Ask gently:

Was the prostitution-related case connected to trafficking, coercion, exploitation, or someone forcing or directing what happened?

If yes, route to manual/legal review under 973.015(2m).
16.
Do not say eligible unless&
rules
Wilma should not say
you are eligible
for adult Wisconsin expungement unless:

age at offense was under 25;
offense carried maximum imprisonment of 6 years or less;
no exclusion applies;
the judge ordered expungement at sentencing;
sentence was successfully completed;
no subsequent conviction occurred before completion;
probation was not revoked;
probation conditions were satisfied.
Wilma should not say DOJ arrest removal is available unless:
the arrest resulted in no charge, dismissal, acquittal, or court clearing;
all charges from the arrest fingerprint event were cleared;
the user can submit fingerprint identification and supporting documentation.
Wilma should not say juvenile expungement is available unless:
the person is at least 17;
the juvenile dispositional order was completed;
the court can find benefit to the juvenile and no harm to society.
